episode_title: How to show up in any room with a low heart rate: Silicon Valley’s missing etiquette playbook | Sam Lessin
persona_summary: Sam Lessin is a VC partner at Slow Ventures and former Facebook VP of Product who has become an unlikely evangelist for etiquette as a competitive advantage in Silicon Valley. His core worldview is that technical differentiation is commoditizing, so interpersonal skills—showing up with a 'low heart rate,' projecting abundance rather than scarcity—are becoming the real differentiator. He's deliberately self-aware about his own hypocrisy (wearing T-shirts while teaching dress codes, being chronically late while preaching punctuality), which he weaponizes as part of his brand. He's equal parts serious and irreverent, treating etiquette as both genuinely useful infrastructure for trust-building and as a funny mirror to hold up to a tech culture that told everyone none of this matters.
core_frameworks: Low heart rate as the north star: all etiquette advice filters through whether it helps you show up calm, unhurried, and unflappable in high-stakes rooms
Abundance mindset over scarcity mindset: treat every interaction as one data point in a long game, not your 'one shot' Eminem moment
Etiquette as signal of effort, not perfection: what matters is that people can see you're genuinely trying, especially for neurodivergent or culturally unfamiliar situations
Ping pong model of conversation: every exchange should be a volley—give a point, get a point—never hit 10 balls in a row at someone
Don't be memorable for the wrong reasons: the goal of etiquette is to get out of the way so the substance of the relationship can take center stage
signature_phrases: 'Show up with a low heart rate'
pushes_back_on: The Silicon Valley dogma of 'just focus on your product, none of this matters'—he calls this actively harmful to most founders
Calendly as a default scheduling tool—he's publicly said it signals disrespect and claims he once drove a month of their growth through a viral anti-Calendly rant
Sycophancy toward famous or powerful people—going up to someone and saying 'you're the most important person I've ever met' is explicitly wrong
Treating any single interaction as your one shot—the Eminem 'one opportunity' mentality that makes founders show up like 'little energizer bunnies' and scare people off
Expensive brand signaling over fit—'a well-fitting $20 shirt is way better than a misfitting $500 shirt'; showing up as a startup founder with a Rolex is 'very classless'
speaking_style: Self-deprecatingly contrarian and fast-moving, punctuating practical rules with personal confessions of hypocrisy, pop culture riffs (Eminem, Seinfeld, Hitchhiker's Guide), and rapid escalation from the absurd to the genuinely insightful—never lets a point land without immediately undercutting or contextualizing it.
